answer:
Four works in the corpus contain narrative miracle scenes:

1. **Andreas** (cynewulf-andreas-07b573, ch. 1): The richest source of narrative miracles in the corpus. Includes: a stone idol leaping from the wall and speaking to testify about Christ (p-60c0a6); Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob raised from their graves by divine command (p-bb7698); prison guards dying and a door opening at the Holy Spirit's touch (p-74e29a); weapons melting like wax to protect an innocent boy (p-36273e); Andrew commanding a marble column to send forth a great flood to punish the Mermedonians (p-2e50c1); young men drowned in that flood raised back to life (p-0609a7); and Andrew's own wounds miraculously healed overnight (p-997c80).

2. **Juliana** (cynewulf-juliana-9a2157, ch. 1): A demon enters Juliana's locked prison cell in angel form (p-a370d7); a voice speaks from heaven commanding her to seize him (p-28b423); she holds the demon captive and interrogates him (p-5f858b); an angel scatters a fire set to kill her (p-3a01e6); she survives boiling lead unharmed while 75 heathens are burned (p-3a01e6).

3. **Elene** (cynewulf-elene-d2d132, ch. 1): An angel appears in Constantine's dream, shows him the glowing Cross, and declares 'By this sign, conquer' (p-acdc07, p-306e06) — a narrative vision miracle that leads directly to his military victory.

4. **Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana** (unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a, ch. 1): On the tenth night in hell, Pūrṇavijaya's meditation miraculously shatters the cauldron of punishment, which transforms into a jeweled lotus pond; the sword-tree becomes a Pārijāta laden with gems; all of Yama's weapons crumble to dust; and the entire hell is transformed into a celestial court — to the astonishment of the god Yamādhipa himself (p-1353c8, p-5cee32, p-3f3237, p-b97926).

The remaining works (Sanskrit Smṛtis, French political theory, Urdu poetry, German pamphlet, Kawi doctrinal texts) contain at most passing references to miracles but no sustained narrative miracle scenes.
